summary: Fowler describes a recurring problem where automated tests delivered with software projects are disabled or deleted by client teams after handover. When Thoughtworks returns for follow-up work, they discover tests have been commented out or excluded from builds because teams removed failing tests rather than investigating them. While acknowledging this as partly a failure to communicate testing's value, Fowler maintains that tests still provide value during initial development even if later abandoned, and notes that some teams do successfully maintain them.
key_points: Client teams often disable or delete automated tests when they fail rather than investigating the failures
Test suites can have as many lines of code as the functional code itself, representing significant investment
The problem is common enough to warrant a name: 'Test Cancer'
Tests still provide value during initial development even if abandoned afterward
Teaching clients the value of tests and how to maintain them remains an unsolved challenge
money_quotes: We ask what happened and are told things like 'we made a change and some tests broke, so we removed the tests'.
Our precious tests are afflicted with a nasty cancer that is time-consuming and frustrating to eradicate.
Failing tests need to be investigated, not simply ignored.
Even if a particularly virulent strain wipes them all out the day after we leave, we still got value from them while we were building the system.
key_insight: Automated tests are only valuable if teams understand that failing tests signal problems to investigate, not inconveniences to delete.
